replies.author: @Simon-xc5oy
replies.text: Yes. Me too. I remember hating it at the time. Trying to fit in at school etc...worries about jobs, what I was going to do for a career when I left. If I could even find work. And all the time in the background....Russia and the threat of nuclear war. Tensions high...then Chernobyl. Nuclear fallout all across Europe and eventually reaching us in the UK. Soon after the wall came down, there were arms control talks and treaties signed. But in the early 80s we were all bricking it expecting to go boom at any time. Threads etc.... The only bit I liked about the 80s was the great movies. Poltergeist, Wrath of Khan, Return of the Jedi, Indiana Jones, Back to the Future, Ghostbusters. And Arnie and Stallone at their peak. Rocky. Rambo, The Terminator, Predator, Running Man and so on...we did not get a video till 1987 as they were so damn expensive for years. But we had one in time to see stuff like Robocop at home when it was released and Platoon and Aliens and Running Man was another iconic Arnie film along with Predator. I was just glad to be there to see them, as the misery in my life and real world of the 80s was depressing as blazes. When you see all the 80s films now from America and all the teens are good looking, pastel coloured clothes, malls,  cool cars,  etc...nope. None of that in my world. It really was as far from Miami Vice as you could get. It looked great on screen though..but the real 80s was crap.

replies.text:
"Arnold movies CANNOT be remade for any reason"

I'd say that it's more or less a truism of most classic movies.

They think that they have to radically reinvent the story to resell it.

But the problem is that those films were classics BECAUSE of the choices made in their production in the first place.

Same problem with American adaptations of manga/anime - Hollywood JUST DOESN'T GET IT.

Almost every single one of them was either dogs**t or a bad copy of an existing Hollywood movie with a thin skin of the adapted IP stretched over it - like ScarJo's Ghost in the Shell which is basically just a bad copy of Robocop, lacking all the geopolitical intrigue and philosophical musings of the IP it's supposed to be adapting.

Alita Battle Angel wasn't QUITE as bad as ScarJo GITS, but they still managed to horribly mangle basically every single character and force bland Hollywood movie tropes onto them to the point that it almost completely defiled the very things about the manga that made me love it in the first place - and at that point why even bother to make it to begin with?

Honestly as much as I enjoy them, I do think that most Arnie movies are pretty basic b1tch (as are the characters he plays in them) and could be remade with the right actor, and a film studio willing to actually make those kinds of films - I just don't think that Hollywood is in that place right now, and may not be again for quite some time to come.

The world is full of big, burly guys - I'm sure at least a handful of them can act AND have enough personality/charm to fill the space in film making that Arnie once did.

All Hollywood needs to do is stop tripping over their own d1cks and never ending greed for long enough to let it happen.

What I really find to be a shame: the book (for me) was not actually about society's "obsession" with violence and reality TV shows. replies.author: @mattpeterson3002
replies.text:
There’s nothing inherently wrong with having a diverse cast IF it serves the premise of the film.

For example, if the movie is about living in a slum, and you have 60% black folk, 35% Latino, and 10% white people, and the PREMISE is about how poverty affects us ALL, then there’s NOTHING wrong with that.

BUT,  if the premise is a group of Chinese scientists launch a space-craft to investigate a strange asteroid, and the cast is composed of a gay Pakistani couple, a Russian girl in a wheelchair, an autistic 9 year old American boy, and 3 twenty-something Chinese men who are brilliant stoner-scientists, then THAT is total B.S., and is featuring diversity only for the sake of being unrealistically inclusive.

Are there people in the world who are gay, trans, disabled, intellectually brilliant? Of course!

But give us stories that feature these characters in REALISTIC SITUATIONS, which can allow us to empathize with them, and not see them as props!

That’s my take.

replies.text: ​@wjzav1971 The cast is supposed to help the audience engage with the story. Just about any fantastical scenario can be explored if the audience cares about the people within the story. The biggest problem with modern Hollywood is their inability to write empathetic characters. They're so busy choosing actors to checkmark boxes that they don't focus on talent. This issue also affects the writing staff...minorities being hired regardless of talent or objective credentials has ensured that most tv and movies reflect the very limited worldview of Liberal elitists. Unsurprisingly, the same topics e.g. racism, patriarchy etc. are constantly inserted into countless stories because these people only care about activism....not quality storytelling. Most characters no longer serve the needs of the story...they're one-dimensional caricatures that exist solely to praise minorities (and women) and demonize white men. It's very gross and very obvious. No one resented diverse casting until it became a pre-requisite that actively discriminated against caucasians. It's really odd that the same people that constantly claim that representation is so necessary for minorities expect caucasians to empathize with all minority characters...the double standard is impossible to ignore. I used to support DEI initiatives until I saw just how much discrimination has been justified by people who think they can socially engineer society. Hollywood has spent the last decade demonizing a large portion of their audience while expecting minorities and women to praise their ridiculous pandering and misandry. You may accept the sort of discrimination that Hollywood peddles but that industry won't survive off the ticket sales of hypocrites.

@smyers9052that is not my opinion at all.
I care whether or not the actor is GOOD, and if they are believable in their role. I dislike producers or execs who cast roles with actors who may cause me to break the suspension of disbelief.

Can a gay man portray a straight police officer? Of course!

Can a trans person portray a scientist? Of course!

But casting a white guy as the leader of the Jamaican Bobsled team, when that is not historically accurate, that affects suspension of disbelief.

I’m not racist, nor do I condone hatred of ANY people group.

author: @AFLOVEable
text:
I might argue, Stephen King could have watched German TV and/or has at least read short-stories from Robert Sheckley.  In 1970, A German broadcaster produced a TV-play called "The million (bucks) game", by adapting such a short-story.  The script went like this:
One player had the chance to win 1 million (a looooot of cash in 1970), if he managed to escape a gang of hired hitmen for 1 week and make it back to the TV-studio on the last day, to collect his money. The population was asked to take part in the game, by either helping him or hand him over to the killers.
Sounds very similar, to what the "brilliant mind" of S. King wrote down, just manipulate a little bit with the motivation, the reward and other minor circumstances  - et voila!
Oh, and surely, the hunt was broadcasted live on TV.

replies.author: @woodrowgodshyne1654
replies.text:
​@tacticalmattfoleyMy best friend was enlisting in the Marines, and I was supposed to go with him, but my GrandPops was a WWII and Korean War vet, and he didn't want me to go into the military. Seriously, Pops punked the Marine recruiter and essentially sent him running from the house while Pops stood on the front porch and waved goodbye. It was effin' funny. After Pops passed, I found out he saw some really horrible things, and he didn't want to chance me going through the same thing. He even tried to talk my best friend out of joining. Didn't work.

So, my big homie was training at Paris Island.... Now his Moms Rosie, Pops Marvin, and Sister Lo were like my family, too. I would go and visit and check up.

One day, I show up and Momma Rosie is running around like a lunatic. She's full-blooded Filipino, so you know she is a bloody ball of angry energy when it comes to her kids.

I hit up Lo and ask what is going the f*ck on. Well, someone called the previos night "claiming" to be my big homie, and he had "violated" in boot camp. Notice the "...

It's not funny, but it kinda is... Let's flip over to my Big Homie's perspectine.

He's on the shooting range when a jeep pulls up. They pick him up and take him to a room and ask, "Just tell what happened".

Big homie is like, "What the f*ck are you talking about!?" He didn't make the call. 

He spent the next 2 days being questioned by the higher-ups at Paris Island for something he didn't do. Hell, he didn't know the nature of the call in the first 36 hours. He was just kept in a room. 

It turned out to be some idiot we went to high school with that was mentally deranged.

Then Big Homie dived on the Marines. He enlisted in the Navy, and my uncle and I chucked when we called me "Seaman".

author: @ZakEmber
text: I don't disagree often with the Drinker, but I do here about this review. This review is a "damned if you do, damned if you don't" catch 22: this film actually tries to follow the book and source material a little more closely, which is something most reviewers, (including the Drinker) tend to say they want because the original artists' vision gets lost in Hollywood and their egos. Well, this movie actually aims to be closer King's work writing as "Richard Bauchman", and have the dark approach, the militaristic hunters, and a longer game of cat-and-moust, but then gets slammed for not being the over-the-top Hollywood Rewrite version that Arnold's was with the cartoonish characters and completely re-written plot that doesn't match the book at all except for borrowing names and being a lethal TV show. I'm not saying the new version is good or great, (the obviously changed the ending from the book and it has its own political agenda), but praising Arnold's version with its radical changes defeats the usual point of trying to compare the film to the original work, which was the book, and using that as a measuring stick as to whether this was a good adaptation.
